cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what we can infer from the Toy Fair 2014 based on the article. The options are A to D. First, I'll read through the article again to make sure I understand the key points. The article mentions that the 2014 Toy Fair had a trend called "Larger Than Life," which is about big toys with big play value. Also, there's a mention of STEAM (science, technology, engineering, art, and math) being a trend, pushing towards education. They talk about word games and high-tech games like 3D games and robots. Looking at the options: A) Toys are becoming more educational than fun. The article says there's a push towards education with STEAM, but it also mentions that there were high-tech games and the "Larger Than Life" trend. So maybe it's not that educational toys are more than fun, but that they're part of the trends along with fun big toys. The article doesn't say they're replacing fun, just adding educational aspects. B) Robots are great favorites of buyers. The article mentions robots as part of the high-tech trend, but does it say they are "great favorites"? It says "we saw some super cool, high-tech games... This year we saw lots of 3D games, and robots." So maybe robots are part of the tech trend, but is there a statement that they are the favorite? Not exactly. The "Larger Than Life" trend was a favorite, but that's about size, not specifically robots. C) Big toys will be popular this year. The article states, "This year's trends include oversize toys. 'In 2014, everything is really big and really out there,' says Adrienne Appell... 'Larger Than Life' was a Toy Fair favorite." So this directly supports that big toys are a trend, so this seems correct. D) Simple card games will disappear on the market. The article mentions that simple card games were shown at the fair, but the trend is towards big and educational. But does that mean simple card games will disappear? The article doesn't say that. It just lists what was shown. So D is probably incorrect. So between the options, C is directly supported by the article.
